fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a terminal command safety guard tool — basically something that sits in front of shell commands and flags dangerous ones before they actually run. Think of it like that risk-filter layer we discussed last quarter but never shipped. The core idea: you define a bunch of rules (some patterns, some more complex), load them from a config file, and when a command comes in it gets checked against whatever rules are active. We also need to be able to manage those rule sets — add groups, remove groups, swap out the confirmation style, and reset everything back to defaults when things get messy. The config stuff is YAML-based, similar to what the ops team is already used to. One thing that kept coming up in the retro: the 'challenge' mechanism needs to be configurable per-deployment, not hardcoded. Also, when you reset to defaults, the system should support optionally keeping a copy of the old config — legal asked about audit trails last week. The default groups after a reset should match what we standardized on in the base rollout (you know the three buckets we settled on). Output formatting needs to be consistent and parseable so the test harness can validate it automatically. Please keep the matching engine decoupled from the config I/O so we can swap pieces independently later.

One extra pass of detail based on the questions that came in: for matching, we only support exactly three methods, 'contains', 'starts_with', and 'regex'. 'contains' is a literal substring match anywhere in the command, 'starts_with' means the command has to begin with the pattern, and 'regex' is a full regular-expression match against the command text. If a check comes through using anything else, treat it as unsupported.

On the config output side, each check line needs to be rendered in one consistent format so it stays machine-parseable: check=<group>|<method>|<enabled>|<challenge_override>|<description>\n. For example, 'check=default-check|Regex|true|Default|'. The method should be title-case in output, so 'Regex' not 'regex', and when there is no per-check override the challenge override should be 'Default'. Also, the description can be blank, but that trailing pipe still needs to be there. The top-level 'challenge' setting in the YAML config is the thing that controls the confirmation prompt style, like 'Math' or 'Yes', and whenever we print config state it should show up as 'challenge=<value>'.

Also clarifying reset behavior a bit more, since that came up a few times. When reset happens, the included groups go back to exactly three defaults, in this order: 'base', 'fs', 'git'. Those are the standardized buckets from the base rollout, and the includes line should come out as 'includes=base,fs,git'. Old checks do not carry forward on reset, so anything from the previous config is dropped. That means the resulting check_count after reset will be 0 if none of the available_checks provided belong to those three default groups. And for the reset output ordering, it needs to start with 'backup_created=yes' if backup_existing is true, or 'backup_created=no' if it is false. That line comes before the challenge, includes, and check lines.

For command evaluation output, the block for each command starts with 'command[<index>]=<command_text>\n' and then 'match_count=<n>\n'. After that, for each matched rule, in order, print 'match_group=<group>\n' and 'match_description=<description>\n'. If there are multiple commands, put a separator line '---\n' after each one except the last. And if a command has no matches, then it only prints the command line and 'match_count=0\n'.

trigger_keywords: how are disabled checks handled during matching
do disabled rules still trigger matches
what happens to checks where enabled is false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Checks with 'enabled: false' (or 'enable: false' in YAML config) must never produce a match, even if their pattern would otherwise match the command text. Only enabled checks participate in command risk evaluation.

trigger_keywords: how does add and remove interact when the same group appears in both
what happens if a group is in both add and remove lists
does remove take priority over add when updating groups
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When updating groups, removals are applied after additions. So if a group appears in both 'add' and 'remove' lists, it will end up excluded from the final configuration. The group will not appear in the includes list and its checks will not be in the active check list.

trigger_keywords: what is the default challenge type
what challenge value is used after reset
what challenge gets set on a fresh configuration
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The default challenge type after a reset is 'Math'. This is the hardcoded default used when resetting configuration regardless of what the previous challenge value was.

trigger_keywords: how is the YAML check's method field capitalized
is the method field case sensitive in YAML
how should method casing be normalized when loading config
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When loading a YAML configuration, the method field from the YAML (e.g., 'Regex', 'regex', 'REGEX') should be preserved or normalized to title-case in the output (e.g., 'Regex'). When checks come from the available_checks catalog (JSON), the method is also normalized to title-case for output display.

trigger_keywords: how should the test harness be invoked
what is the test runner entry point
how do I run the full test suite
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test suite is executed via 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh'. It accepts an optional '--cases-dir <subdir>' argument (default: 'public_test_cases'). For each case it writes stdout to 'rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_index>.txt'. The adapter is invoked by piping JSON to stdin and capturing stdout. Pass/fail is determined by exact string match between captured stdout and expected_output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what the ops team is already used to
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The YAML configuration schema used by the tool, with fields: 'challenge', 'includes' (list), 'version', and 'checks' (list of objects with 'from', 'test', 'method', 'enable', 'description' keys), as shown in the initial_yaml samples across feature2_* test cases.

context_pointers.vague_reference: that risk-filter layer we discussed last quarter but never shipped
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The command matching engine (Feature 1: match_commands) that evaluates shell command strings against a list of RiskCheck objects using contains, starts_with, or regex methods, returning structured match results per command.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can clarify off the top of my head — you'll want to look at the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference with the expected_output strings there. The contracts are fully specified in those JSON files.

feature1_1_contains_matching.json.description: A command is reported as risky when it contains an enabled literal pattern, and non-matching text reports no matched risk.

cases.expected_output:
command[0]=test is valid
match_count=1
match_group=sample
match_description=contains matched
---
command[1]=not-found
match_count=0

feature1_2_prefix_matching.json.description: A command is reported as risky only when it begins with an enabled prefix pattern.

cases.expected_output:
command[0]=test is valid
match_count=1
match_group=sample
match_description=prefix matched
---
command[1]=is
match_count=0

feature1_3_regex_matching.json.description: A command is reported as risky when an enabled regular-expression pattern matches the command text.

cases.expected_output:
command[0]=rm -rf
match_count=1
match_group=sample
match_description=regex matched
---
command[1]=foo
match_count=0

feature2_1_load_configuration.json.description: A YAML configuration is loaded into challenge type, included risk groups, and enabled check summaries.

input.yaml:
---
challenge: Math
includes:
  - default-check
version: 0.2.2
checks:
- from: default-check
  test: default-check
  method: Regex
  enable: true
  description: ""

feature2_2_update_check_groups.json.description: Adding risk groups inserts their default checks without duplicating existing includes, while removing groups deletes their checks from the active configuration.

input.initial_yaml:
---
challenge: Math
includes:
  - default-check
version: 0.2.2
checks:
- from: default-check
  test: default-check
  method: Regex
  enable: true
  description: ""

cases.expected_output:
challenge=Math
includes=default-check,test-1,test-2
check_count=3
check=default-check|Regex|true|Default|
check=test-1|Regex|true|Default|
check=test-2|Regex|true|Default|

input.initial_yaml:
---
challenge: Math
includes:
  - default-check
version: 0.2.2
checks:
- from: default-check
  test: default-check
  method: Regex
  enable: true
  description: ""

input.initial_yaml:
---
challenge: Math
includes:
  - default-check
version: 0.2.2
checks:
- from: default-check
  test: default-check
  method: Regex
  enable: true
